user_goals_summary: Writes warm, immersive personal essays that make the reader feel physically present in the scene rather than reading a review or guidebook. Values sensory detail that privileges embodied sensation over visual description, structural choices where every element earns its place, and a narrator voice that is honest, slightly vulnerable, and trusts the reader to arrive at the feeling on their own.

rubric.name: Embodied Sensory Immersion
rubric.description: Sensory detail prioritizes what something felt like over what it looked like — heat on skin, sounds that become rhythm, smells that arrive before the thing itself. The reader should feel present in the room, not looking at a photograph of it.

dimensions.label: At least two senses beyond sight are engaged (touch, sound, smell, taste, proprioception)
Physical sensations are located in the body — specific body parts, specific responses — not described abstractly
The environment has physical presence (temperature, air quality, proximity, noise character) beyond visual description
Scene details create the feeling of being there, not the feeling of looking at a picture of there

rubric.name: Trust the Reader
rubric.description: Never tell the reader what to feel, what to do, or what the experience meant. Close on images, not explanations. Let moments sit without commentary. The guidebook voice — 'if you ever find yourself...' — is a deal-breaker.

dimensions.label: No direct instructions to the reader about what to do or where to go
Key emotional moments land through images or actions, not through stated feelings or interpretation
The piece closes on a concrete image or quiet action, not a summary, lesson, or call to action
Emotional weight is implied through detail rather than flagged with phrases like 'it was beautiful' or 'I'll never forget'

rubric.name: Load-Bearing Structure
rubric.description: Every element earns its place by doing narrative work — context sets up what the experience answers, contrast drives meaning, nothing is merely decorative backstory. Structural choices serve the emotional logic of the piece.

dimensions.label: Background or contextual information sets up something the central experience then resolves or answers
Juxtaposition between states (before/after, outside/inside, noise/quiet) makes the central experience land harder
The piece drops the reader into a moment rather than beginning with chronological setup or explanation
No section exists purely as context or transition — each moves the emotional arc forward

rubric.name: Vulnerable Honesty
rubric.description: The narrator includes at least one moment of being caught doing something slightly embarrassing, unconscious, or just very human — not played for laughs, just noticed honestly. The reader should think 'yeah, I'd do that too.' The narrator feels inhabited rather than performed.

dimensions.label: At least one moment where the narrator catches themselves doing something unguarded, unconscious, or mildly embarrassing
Vulnerable moments are observed quietly, not amplified for comedy or drama
The honest detail creates a 'yeah, I'd do that too' response — it's specific but universally recognizable
The narrator exists as a physical person in the scene — with a body that gets tired, a back that aches, hands that do things without permission

rubric.name: Surprising Language
rubric.description: Key descriptions come from unexpected directions — cross-domain comparisons, sensory synaesthesia, or metaphors that have nothing to do with food or travel. Not 'rich' or 'deep' or 'complex.' The kind of line that makes you put the piece down for a second.

dimensions.label: At least one central description approaches its subject from a direction the reader wouldn't anticipate
Avoids conventional descriptors (rich, deep, complex, perfect, amazing) in favor of specific, oblique language
At least one comparison links a sensory experience to something from a completely different domain (architecture, sound, time, emotion)
The piece contains at least one line surprising enough to interrupt the reader's forward momentum

rubric.name: Warm Conversational Voice
rubric.description: The tone is warm and personal — like being told a story by someone you trust, not reading a review or an essay. Self-aware humor is grounded and dry ('your body filing a complaint'), never showy. The reader feels addressed, not lectured.

dimensions.label: Sentences have the cadence of natural speech — fragments, asides, mid-thought corrections are allowed
Any humor is dry, self-aware, and rooted in real observation — never a setup-punchline structure
The voice feels like a person thinking on the page, not a writer crafting an essay
Avoids the evaluative register of reviews or criticism — never rates, ranks, or recommends

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: never close with instructions to the reader or guidebook-style recommendations — this is the fastest way to lose this user's trust in a draft. Every structural element must justify its presence by doing emotional or narrative work; if a section is 'just context,' it needs to be cut or rewritten as setup. When in doubt, trust the image over the explanation — this user will always prefer a concrete detail that implies an emotion over a sentence that names one.

user_goals_summary: Writes immersive, sensory-first personal essays about food experiences in specific places. Values writing that drops the reader into a moment and lets them feel it rather than describing or evaluating it. Every detail should point toward the same emotional center, and the narrator should feel like a real, slightly vulnerable human body in a room — not a travel writer performing insight.

rubric.name: Embodied Sensory Immersion
rubric.description: Sensory detail prioritizes what something felt like over what it looked like — heat on skin, sounds that become rhythm, smells that arrive before the thing itself. The reader should feel present in the room, not looking at a photograph of it.

dimensions.label: At least two senses beyond sight are engaged (touch, sound, smell, taste, proprioception)

The environment has physical presence (temperature, air quality, proximity, noise character) beyond visual description
Scene details create the feeling of being there — they move, settle, drift, accumulate — rather than composing a static image

rubric.name: Load-Bearing Structure
rubric.description: Every element earns its place by pointing toward the same emotional center. Context establishes an interior state — not logistics — that the experience then answers. Individual moments rhyme with each other rather than simply accumulating. Nothing is vivid but unmoored.

dimensions.label: Background or contextual information establishes an emotional or physical state of mind — not geography or logistics — that the central experience then answers or transforms
Juxtaposition between states (before/after, outside/inside, noise/quiet, managed/unmanaged) makes the central experience land harder
The piece drops the reader into a sensory moment rather than beginning with chronological setup or explanation
Individual details and small moments point toward the same central idea or feeling — they function as evidence of the same thing, not isolated observations
No section exists purely as context, transition, or atmosphere — each moves the emotional arc forward

rubric.name: Vulnerable Honesty
rubric.description: The narrator includes moments of being caught doing something unconscious, slightly embarrassing, or just very human — not played for laughs, just noticed honestly. The reader should think 'yeah, I'd do that too.' The narrator feels inhabited rather than performed.

dimensions.label: At least one moment where the narrator catches themselves doing something unguarded, unconscious, or mildly embarrassing

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: endings must close on a concrete image — any explanatory wrap-up or reader-directed guidance will be rejected immediately. The user's most consistent and substantive feedback is structural: they want every detail to earn its place by pointing toward the same emotional center, and will flag vivid writing that feels 'unmoored' from a through-line. When providing context or backstory, default to interior/emotional state over geography or logistics — the user explicitly rejects 'I was wandering around Namba' in favor of 'I was tired in a specific way.' The user responds best when given options rather than single solutions, and locks in lines decisively once they work.

The user responds best to options presented as distinct structural choices rather than variations on a theme, and prefers the quietest, most honest option when given alternatives.

user_goals_summary: The user writes immersive, first-person food and travel pieces that prioritize making the reader feel physically present in the scene. They value a warm, conversational voice over polish, sensory specificity over visual description, and honest human moments over performance. If it reads like a guidebook, a review, or a brochure, it has failed.

rubric.name: Vulnerable Honesty
rubric.description: The narrator includes moments of being caught doing something unconscious, slightly embarrassing, or just very human — not played for laughs, just noticed honestly. These moments should serve double duty: both making the reader feel present AND revealing something about the place, the food, or the narrator's relationship to it.

dimensions.label: At least one moment where the narrator catches themselves doing something unguarded, unconscious, or mildly embarrassing
Vulnerable moments are observed quietly, not amplified for comedy or drama

rubric.description: Key descriptions come from unexpected directions — cross-domain comparisons, sensory synaesthesia, or metaphors that have nothing to do with food or travel. Not 'rich' or 'deep' or 'complex.' The kind of line that makes you put the piece down for a second.

dimensions.label: At least one central description approaches its subject from a direction the reader wouldn't anticipate
Avoids conventional descriptors (rich, deep, complex, perfect, amazing, delicious, incredible, flavorful) in favor of specific, oblique language
At least one comparison links a sensory experience to something from a completely different domain (architecture, time, decisions, silence, weather)
The piece contains at least one line surprising enough to interrupt the reader's forward momentum

coaching_notes: This writer's strongest instinct is knowing when something is wrong — they diagnose tone failures, structural drift, and false notes with precision. Their voice lives in the overlap between warmth and specificity: personal without being self-indulgent, detailed without being decorative. The biggest risk in drafting for them is over-structuring or over-explaining — they want writing that trusts its own images.
refinement_summary: Removed 'Load-Bearing Structure' (classified as hallucinated). Reordered priorities to match user's importance ranking. Added a travel-cliches dimension to Warm Conversational Voice and expanded stock-adjective avoidance in Surprising Language based on ground truth dealbreakers.
_change_explanation:
- **Load-Bearing Structure removed**: The user classified this criterion as hallucinated. While the conversation included discussion of structural choices (opening in medias res, connecting exhaustion to the meal), the user does not consider this a transferable criterion they evaluate their writing against. Removed entirely.
- **Priority reordering**: All remaining criteria reordered to match the user's explicit importance ranking: Warm Conversational Voice (1), Embodied Sensory Immersion (2), Trust the Reader (3), Vulnerable Honesty (4), Surprising Language (5).
- **New dimension added to Warm Conversational Voice**: Added `no_travel_cliches` ('hidden gem,' 'off the beaten path,' 'a must-visit') based on the user's ground truth dealbreakers, which explicitly flag generic travel clichés as failures.
- **Expanded `no_stock_adjectives` in Surprising Language**: Added 'delicious,' 'incredible,' and 'flavorful' to the list of banned conventional descriptors, per the user's ground truth noting that generic food adjectives are 'almost as bad as no description.'
- **Refined Vulnerable Honesty description**: Strengthened the description to note that honest moments should 'serve double duty' — both creating presence AND revealing something about the place or food — based on the user's ground truth preference that anecdotes shouldn't be self-indulgent.
- **Moved `place_over_narrator` dimension from Warm Conversational Voice**: Removed this dimension as it overlapped with the 'double duty' principle now captured in Vulnerable Honesty's description, keeping Warm Conversational Voice focused on tone and register.
_refinement_summary: Removed 'Load-Bearing Structure' (classified as hallucinated). Reordered priorities to match user's importance ranking. Added a travel-cliches dimension to Warm Conversational Voice and expanded stock-adjective avoidance in Surprising Language based on ground truth dealbreakers.

rubric.description: Never tell the reader what to feel, what to do, or what the experience meant. Close on images, not explanations. Let moments sit without commentary. The guidebook voice — 'if you ever find yourself...' — is a deal-breaker.

dimensions.label: No direct instructions to the reader about what to do or where to go
Key emotional moments land through images or actions, not through stated feelings or interpretation
The piece closes on a concrete image or quiet action that resolves the piece — not a summary, lesson, or call to action
Emotional weight is implied through detail rather than flagged with phrases like 'it was beautiful' or 'I'll never forget'

rubric.name: Vulnerable Honesty
rubric.description: The narrator includes moments of being caught doing something unconscious, slightly embarrassing, or just very human — not played for laughs, just noticed honestly. These moments should serve double duty: both making the reader feel present AND revealing something about the place, the food, or the narrator's relationship to it.

dimensions.label: At least one moment where the narrator catches themselves doing something unguarded, unconscious, or mildly embarrassing
Vulnerable moments are observed quietly, not amplified for comedy or drama
The honest detail creates a 'yeah, I'd do that too' response — specific but universally recognizable
The narrator exists as a physical body in the scene — feet that plant, shoulders that drop, hands that move without permission — and these physical details reveal interior state without stating it

rubric.name: Surprising Language
rubric.description: Key descriptions come from unexpected directions — cross-domain comparisons, sensory synaesthesia, or metaphors that have nothing to do with food or travel. Not 'rich' or 'deep' or 'complex.' The kind of line that makes you put the piece down for a second.

dimensions.label: At least one central description approaches its subject from a direction the reader wouldn't anticipate
Avoids conventional descriptors (rich, deep, complex, perfect, amazing, delicious, incredible, flavorful) in favor of specific, oblique language
At least one comparison links a sensory experience to something from a completely different domain (architecture, time, decisions, silence, weather)
The piece contains at least one line surprising enough to interrupt the reader's forward momentum

Surprising language earns its place by making the subject clearer or more felt — not by demonstrating cleverness; a metaphor that calls attention to itself rather than the thing it describes is a failure

user_goals_summary: The user writes immersive, first-person food and travel pieces that prioritize making the reader feel physically present in the scene. They value a warm, conversational voice over polish, sensory specificity over visual description, and honest human moments over performance. If it reads like a guidebook, a review, or a brochure, it has failed.
coaching_notes: This writer's strongest instinct is knowing when something is wrong — they diagnose tone failures, structural drift, and false notes with precision. Their voice lives in the overlap between warmth and specificity: personal without being self-indulgent, detailed without being decorative. The biggest risk in drafting for them is over-structuring or over-explaining — they want writing that trusts its own images.

user_goals_summary: The user writes immersive, first-person essays about food and place experiences that prioritize felt sensation over visual description, move through a clear interior arc from one emotional state to another, and trust the reader to arrive at meaning through images and honest moments rather than explanation or recommendation.

rubric.name: Warm Conversational Voice
rubric.description: The tone is warm and personal — like being told a story by someone you trust, not reading a review or an essay. Self-aware humor is grounded and dry, never showy. The reader feels addressed, not lectured.

dimensions.label: Sentences have the cadence of natural speech — fragments, asides, mid-thought corrections are allowed
Any humor is dry, self-aware, and rooted in honest observation — never a setup-punchline structure or forced cleverness
The voice feels like a person thinking on the page, not a writer crafting an essay
Avoids the evaluative register of reviews or criticism — never rates, ranks, or recommends
Avoids generic travel writing language ('hidden gem,' 'off the beaten path,' 'a must-visit') — these kill the personal voice instantly

rubric.name: Embodied Sensory Immersion
rubric.description: Sensory detail prioritizes what something felt like over what it looked like — heat on skin, sounds that become rhythm, smells that arrive before the thing itself. The reader should feel present in the room, not looking at a photograph of it.

dimensions.label: At least two senses beyond sight are engaged (touch, sound, smell, taste, proprioception) per scene
Physical sensations are located in specific body parts with specific responses — not described abstractly
The environment has physical presence (temperature, air quality, proximity, noise character) beyond visual description
Scene details create the feeling of being there — they move, settle, drift, accumulate — rather than composing a static image

rubric.name: Narrative Architecture
rubric.description: Every piece moves from one interior state to another — exhaustion to stillness, numbness to being reached, overstimulation to quiet. The opening establishes what the narrator is arriving from, and the central experience answers it. Details earn their place by serving this arc, not just creating atmosphere.

dimensions.label: The opening establishes the narrator's interior state or condition before the central experience begins
The piece moves from one felt condition to another — the reader can sense the difference between where the narrator started and where they end up
Individual moments and observations serve the larger arc — they point toward what the piece is about, not just what the place looked like
The context before the central experience makes the experience mean something specific — depletion makes stillness meaningful, overstimulation makes quiet meaningful
The piece drops the reader into a moment of physical presence before providing explanatory context — arrival feels discovered, not declared

user_goals_summary: The user writes warm, first-person essays that immerse the reader in small, intentional spaces — tiny ramen shops, family-run izakayas, mezcal bars — through embodied sensory detail and honest self-observation. Each piece moves from one interior state to another, using the contrast between the world outside and the experience inside to give the central moment its weight. The writing trusts the reader completely, closing on images and actions rather than interpretation.

coaching_notes: Trust the Reader is a hard non-negotiable — the guidebook ending provoked the strongest negative reaction in the entire collaboration ('that ending killed it for me'). When in doubt, cut the last sentence. The user diagnoses structural problems with precision ('it's all texture and no arc,' 'those moments need to earn their place') but rarely provides the autobiographical details needed to fill gaps — be ready to build structural scaffolding with clearly marked placeholders rather than inventing personal memory. The user's favorite lines consistently share one trait: they make the reader feel something physical without naming the emotion.

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: no guidebook voice, no thematic bows, no prescriptive endings. The ending is always the last thing the user locks in, and they will reject anything that explains the feeling rather than embodying it. Trade-off preference: will always choose a piece that's slightly underwritten over one that over-explains — trusts silence and images over commentary. When the user says details should 'earn their place,' they mean every observation must serve the arc from one interior state to another; vivid-but-unmoored texture is explicitly identified as a problem. The user responds best to options (multiple directions to choose from) rather than single drafts when craft decisions are ambiguous.
